3D Chromosome Regulatory Landscape of Human Pluripotent Cells. In this study, we describe the 3D chromosome regulatory landscape of human naive and primed embryonic stem cells (ESCs). To devise this map, we identified transcriptional enhancers (DNA sequences that increase gene expression) and insulators (DNA elements that block the effects of enhancers on promoters) in these cells and placed them within the context of cohesin-associated CTCF-CTCF loops (loops formed by the looping out of DNA segments by the CTCF protein and its binding sites) using cohesin ChIA-PET data (a technique that combines chromatin immunoprecipitation with paired-end tag sequencing to map protein-DNA interactions). The CTCF-CTCF loops we identified form a chromosomal framework of insulated neighborhoods (regions of the genome that are isolated from the influence of enhancers outside their boundaries), which in turn form topologically associating domains (TADs) (larger chromosomal domains that associate with each other in 3D space) that are largely preserved during the transition between the naive and primed states (two distinct states of pluripotency in ESCs). Regulatory changes in enhancer-promoter interactions occur within insulated neighborhoods during cell state transition. The CTCF anchor regions we identified are conserved across species (found in similar locations in different organisms), influence gene expression, and are a frequent site of mutations in cancer cells, underscoring their functional importance in cellular regulation. These 3D regulatory maps of human pluripotent cells therefore provide a foundation for future interrogation of the relationships between chromosome structure and gene control in development and disease.

Autoimmunity due to molecular mimicry as a cause of neurological disease One hypothesis that couples infection with autoimmune disease is molecular mimicry (immune response to an environmental agent that cross-reacts with a host antigen, leading to disease). This hypothesis has been implicated in the pathogenesis of diabetes, lupus and multiple sclerosis (MS). There is limited direct evidence linking causative agents with pathogenic immune reactions in these diseases. Our study establishes a clear link between viral infection, autoimmunity and neurological disease in humans. As a model for molecular mimicry, we studied patients with human T-lymphotropic virus type 1 (HTLV-1; a retrovirus that infects CD4+ T cells) -associated myelopathy/tropical spastic paraparesis (HAM/TSP, a disease that can be indistinguishable from MS (refs. 5,6,7)). HAM/TSP patients develop antibodies to neurons. We hypothesized these antibodies would identify a central nervous system (CNS, the brain and spinal cord) autoantigen. Immunoglobulin G (IgG, a type of antibody) isolated from HAM/TSP patients identified heterogeneous nuclear ribonuclear protein-A1 (hnRNP-A1) as the autoantigen. Antibodies to hnRNP-A1 cross-reacted with HTLV-1-tax (a viral protein encoded by the tax gene of HTLV-1), the immune response to which is associated with HAM/TSP (refs. 5,9). Immunoglobulin G specifically stained human Betz cells, whose axons are preferentially damaged. Infusion of autoantibodies in brain sections inhibited neuronal firing, indicative of their pathogenic nature. These data demonstrate the importance of molecular mimicry in the pathogenesis of neurological diseases.

Altered recognition of antigen is a mechanism of CD8+ T cell tolerance in cancer (a state where CD8+ T cells fail to recognize and eliminate cancer cells). Antigen-specific CD8+ T-cell tolerance, induced by myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs; a type of immune cell that promotes tumor growth and suppresses anti-tumor immunity), is one of the main mechanisms of tumor escape. Using in vivo models, we show here that MDSCs directly disrupt the binding of specific peptide–major histocompatibility complex (pMHC; a molecule on the surface of cells that presents antigens to T cells) dimers to CD8-expressing T cells through nitration of tyrosines in a T-cell receptor (TCR; a protein on the surface of T cells that recognizes antigens presented by pMHC)–CD8 complex. This process makes CD8-expressing T cells unable to bind pMHC and to respond to the specific peptide, although they retain their ability to respond to nonspecific stimulation. Nitration of TCR-CD8 is induced by MDSCs through hyperproduction of reactive oxygen species and peroxynitrite (toxic molecules produced during inflammation) during direct cell-cell contact. Molecular modeling suggests specific sites of nitration that might affect the conformational flexibility of TCR-CD8 and its interaction with pMHC. These data identify a previously unknown mechanism of T-cell tolerance in cancer (where T cells are unable to effectively recognize and destroy cancer cells) that is also pertinent to many pathological conditions associated with accumulation of MDSCs.

Neutrophils rapidly undergo polarization and directional movement to infiltrate the sites of infection and inflammation. Here, we show that an inhibitory MHC I receptor, Ly49Q, was crucial for the swift polarization of and tissue infiltration by neutrophils. During the steady state, Ly49Q inhibited neutrophil adhesion by preventing focal-complex formation, likely by inhibiting Src and PI3 kinases (enzymes involved in signal transduction). However, in the presence of inflammatory stimuli, Ly49Q mediated rapid neutrophil polarization and tissue infiltration in an ITIM-domain-dependent manner (ITIMs are immunoreceptor tyrosine-based inhibition motifs found in the cytoplasmic tails of certain receptors). These opposite functions appeared to be mediated by distinct use of effector phosphatase SHP-1 and SHP-2 (SHP-1 and SHP-2 are enzymes that dephosphorylate other proteins, affecting cellular signaling). Ly49Q-dependent polarization and migration were affected by Ly49Q regulation of membrane raft functions (lipid-rich microdomains in the plasma membrane that play a role in cell signaling and trafficking). Impact of diabetes on long-term prognosis in patients with unstable angina and non-Q-wave myocardial infarction: results of the OASIS (Organization to Assess Strategies for Ischemic Syndromes) Registry. BACKGROUND Although unstable coronary artery disease (CAD, narrowing or blockage of arteries supplying blood to the heart muscle) is the most common reason for admission to a coronary care unit (CCU, specialized unit for treating heart conditions), the long-term prognosis (expected course of illness) of patients with this diagnosis is unknown. This is particularly true for patients with diabetes mellitus (DM, condition where the body cannot properly regulate blood sugar levels), who are known to have a high morbidity and mortality (illness and death) after an acute myocardial infarction (AMI, sudden blockage of blood flow to part of the heart muscle). METHODS AND RESULTS Prospectively collected data from 6 different countries in the Organization to Assess Strategies for Ischemic Syndromes (OASIS) registry were analyzed to determine the 2-year prognosis (expected course of illness over 2 years) of diabetic and nondiabetic patients who were hospitalized with unstable angina (chest pain due to insufficient blood supply to the heart) or non-Q-wave myocardial infarction (MI, damage to heart muscle without typical ECG changes). Overall, 1718 of 8013 registry patients (21%) had diabetes. Diabetic patients had a higher rate of coronary bypass surgery (surgical procedure to improve blood flow to the heart by creating new pathways around blocked arteries) than nondiabetic patients (23% versus 20%, P:<0.001) but had similar rates of catheterization (procedure using a thin tube to examine or treat blood vessels) and angioplasty (procedure to widen narrowed or blocked blood vessels). Diabetes independently predicted mortality (RR, 1.57; 95% CI, 1.38 to 1.81; P:<0.001), as well as cardiovascular death, new myocardial infarction, stroke, and new congestive heart failure. ADAR1 (adenosine deaminase acting on RNA) forms a complex with Dicer (a ribonuclease III enzyme involved in microRNA processing) to promote microRNA (miRNA) processing and RNA-induced gene silencing (RNAi). In this study, we investigated the interaction of the RNA editing mechanism with the RNA interference (RNAi) machinery and found that ADAR1 forms a complex with Dicer through direct protein-protein interaction. Most importantly, ADAR1 increases the maximum rate (Vmax) of pre-microRNA (pre-miRNA) cleavage by Dicer and facilitates loading of miRNA onto RNA-induced silencing complexes, identifying a new role of ADAR1 in miRNA processing and RNAi mechanisms. ADAR1 differentiates its functions in RNA editing and RNAi by the formation of either ADAR1/ADAR1 homodimer or Dicer/ADAR1 heterodimer complexes, respectively. As expected, the expression of miRNAs is globally inhibited in ADAR1(-/-) mouse embryos, which, in turn, alters the expression of their target genes and might contribute to their embryonic lethal phenotype.

Exome sequencing identifies somatic gain-of-function PPM1D mutations (acquired changes in the PPM1D gene that increase its function) in brainstem gliomas (GBM arising in the brainstem). Gliomas arising in the brainstem and thalamus are devastating tumors that are difficult to surgically resect (remove through surgery). To determine the genetic and epigenetic landscape (the combination of genetic makeup and environmental factors that influence gene expression) of these tumors, we performed exomic sequencing of 14 brainstem gliomas (BSGs) and 12 thalamic gliomas. We also performed targeted mutational analysis (specific examination for certain genetic mutations) of an additional 24 such tumors and genome-wide methylation profiling (analysis of DNA methylation patterns across the entire genome) of 45 gliomas. This study led to the discovery of tumor-specific mutations in PPM1D, encoding wild-type p53-induced protein phosphatase 1D (WIP1), in 37.5% of the BSGs that harbored hallmark H3F3A mutations encoding p. Lys27Met substitutions. PPM1D mutations were mutually exclusive with TP53 mutations in BSG and attenuated p53 activation in vitro (in laboratory conditions). PPM1D mutations were truncating alterations in exon 6 that enhanced the ability of PPM1D to suppress the activation of the DNA damage response checkpoint protein CHK2. These results define PPM1D as a frequent target of somatic mutation (acquired during a person's lifetime) and as a potential therapeutic target in brainstem gliomas.

Regulation of ADMP (adrenomedullin) and BMP2/4/7 (bone morphogenetic proteins 2, 4, and 7) at opposite embryonic poles generates a self-regulating morphogenetic field (spatially organized concentration of signaling molecules). Embryos have the ability to self-regulate and regenerate normal structures after being sectioned in half. How is such a morphogenetic field established? We discovered that quadruple knockdown of ADMP and BMP2/4/7 in Xenopus embryos eliminates self-regulation, causing ubiquitous neural induction throughout the ectoderm (outermost embryonic layer). ADMP transcription in the Spemann organizer (region of cells that plays a central role in organogenesis) is activated at low BMP levels. When ventral BMP2/4/7 signals are depleted, Admp expression increases, allowing for self-regulation. ADMP has BMP-like activity and signals via the ALK-2 receptor (activin-like kinase 2, a transmembrane serine/threonine kinase receptor). It is unable to signal dorsally because of inhibition by Chordin (a secreted protein that inhibits BMP signaling). The ventral BMP antagonists Sizzled (a secreted protein that inhibits BMP signaling) and Bambi (a secreted protein that inhibits BMP signaling) further refine the pattern. By transplanting dorsal or ventral wild-type grafts into ADMP/BMP2/4/7-depleted hosts, we demonstrate that both poles serve as signaling centers that can induce histotypic differentiation (formation of specific tissue types) over considerable distances. We conclude that dorsal and ventral BMP signals and their extracellular antagonists expressed under opposing transcriptional regulation provide a molecular mechanism for embryonic self-regulation.

Renal considerations in angiotensin converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor therapy: a statement for healthcare professionals from the Council on the Kidney in Cardiovascular Disease and the Council for High Blood Pressure Research of the American Heart Association. Angiotensin converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors are now one of the most frequently used classes of antihypertensive drugs. Beyond their utility in the management of hypertension, their use has been extended to the long-term management of patients with congestive heart failure (CHF; a condition characterized by reduced cardiac output and fluid overload), as well as diabetic and nondiabetic nephropathies (kidney diseases). Although ACE inhibitor therapy usually improves renal blood flow (RBF; the volume of blood flowing through the kidneys per unit time) and sodium excretion rates in CHF and reduces the rate of progressive renal injury in chronic renal disease, its use can also be associated with a syndrome of “functional renal insufficiency” and/or hyperkalemia (elevated potassium levels in the blood). This form of acute renal failure (ARF; a sudden loss of kidney function) most commonly develops shortly after initiation of ACE inhibitor therapy but can be observed after months or years of therapy, even in the absence of prior ill effects. ARF is most likely to occur when renal perfusion pressure (the pressure at which blood flows into the kidneys) cannot be sustained because of substantial decreases in mean arterial pressure (MAP; the average pressure in systemic arteries over the cardiac cycle) or when glomerular filtration rate (GFR; the volume of fluid filtered per minute by the glomeruli in the kidneys) is highly angiotensin II (Ang II; a peptide hormone that constricts blood vessels and increases blood pressure) dependent. Conditions that predict an adverse hemodynamic effect of ACE inhibitors in patients with CHF are preexisting hypotension (low blood pressure) and low cardiac filling pressures (reduced volume of blood returning to the heart). A culture-independent sequence-based metagenomics approach to the investigation of an outbreak of Shiga-toxigenic Escherichia coli O104:H4. IMPORTANCE Identification of the bacterium responsible for an outbreak can aid in disease management (e.g., informing public health interventions and treatment strategies). However, traditional culture-based diagnosis can be difficult, particularly if no specific diagnostic test is available for an outbreak strain (e.g., due to genetic variability among strains). OBJECTIVE To explore the potential of metagenomics, which is the direct sequencing of DNA extracted from microbiologically complex samples (e.g., stool samples), as an open-ended clinical discovery platform capable of identifying and characterizing bacterial strains from an outbreak without laboratory culture. DESIGN, SETTING, AND PATIENTS In a retrospective investigation, 45 samples were selected from fecal specimens obtained from patients with diarrhea during the 2011 outbreak of Shiga-toxigenic Escherichia coli (STEC; a type of E. coli that produces Shiga toxin) O104:H4 in Germany. Samples were subjected to high-throughput sequencing (August-September 2012), followed by a 3-phase analysis (November 2012-February 2013). In phase 1, a de novo assembly approach was developed to obtain a draft genome of the outbreak strain. In phase 2, the depth of coverage of the outbreak strain genome was determined in each sample. In phase 3, sequences from each sample were compared with sequences from known bacteria to identify pathogens other than the outbreak strain. MAIN OUTCOMES AND MEASURES The recovery of genome sequence data for the purposes of identification and characterization (e.g., determining the presence and prevalence of specific bacterial strains) of the outbreak strain and other potential pathogens.

Mechanisms of Life Span Extension by Rapamycin in the Fruit Fly Drosophila melanogaster The target of rapamycin (TOR) pathway is a major nutrient-sensing pathway that, when genetically downregulated, increases life span in evolutionarily diverse organisms including mammals (organisms from different species). The central component of this pathway, TOR kinase, is the target of the inhibitory drug rapamycin, a highly specific and well-described drug approved for human use. We show here that feeding rapamycin to adult Drosophila produces the life span extension seen in some TOR mutants. Increase in life span by rapamycin was associated with increased resistance to both starvation (absence of food) and paraquat (a herbicide known to cause oxidative stress). Analysis of the underlying mechanisms revealed that rapamycin increased longevity specifically through the TORC1 branch of the TOR pathway, through alterations to both autophagy (process of breaking down and recycling cellular components) and translation (process of synthesizing proteins from messenger RNA). Rapamycin could increase life span of weak insulin/Igf signaling (IIS) pathway mutants and of flies with life span maximized by dietary restriction, indicating additional mechanisms.

Antibiotic-induced shifts in the mouse gut microbiome and metabolome increase susceptibility to Clostridium difficile infection (C. difficile, a pathogen causing severe diarrhea). Antibiotics can have significant and long-lasting effects on the gastrointestinal tract microbiota (microbial community in the gut), reducing colonization resistance against pathogens including C. difficile. Here we show that antibiotic treatment induces substantial changes in the gut microbial community and in the metabolome (set of small molecules produced by or essential to the metabolism of an organism) of mice susceptible to C. difficile infection. Levels of secondary bile acids (bile acids produced by bacteria in the gut), glucose, free fatty acids and dipeptides decrease, whereas those of primary bile acids (bile acids synthesized in the liver) and sugar alcohols increase, reflecting the modified metabolic activity of the altered gut microbiome. In vitro (performed in a test tube or culture dish) and ex vivo (performed outside the living body but in a natural environment) analyses demonstrate that C. difficile can exploit specific metabolites that become more abundant in the mouse gut after antibiotics, including the primary bile acid taurocholate (a bile acid with a sulfate group attached to its cholic acid structure) for germination, and carbon sources such as mannitol (a sugar alcohol), fructose, sorbitol (a sugar alcohol), raffinose (a trisaccharide composed of galactose, fructose, and glucose), and stachyose (a tetrasaccharide composed of four glucose units) for growth. Our results indicate that antibiotic-mediated alteration of the gut microbiome converts the global metabolic profile to one that favours C. difficile germination and growth.

Activating mutations in the NT5C2 nucleotidase gene (gene encoding a 5'-nucleotidase enzyme that inactivates nucleoside-analog chemotherapy drugs) drive chemotherapy resistance in relapsed ALL (Acute lymphoblastic leukemia, an aggressive hematological tumor resulting from the malignant transformation of lymphoid progenitors). Despite intensive chemotherapy, 20% of pediatric patients and over 50% of adult patients with ALL do not achieve a complete remission or relapse after intensified chemotherapy, making disease relapse and resistance to therapy the most substantial challenge in the treatment of this disease. Using whole-exome sequencing, we identify mutations in the cytosolic 5'-nucleotidase II gene (NT5C2), which encodes a 5'-nucleotidase enzyme that is responsible for the inactivation of nucleoside-analog chemotherapy drugs, in 20/103 (19%) relapse T cell ALLs and 1/35 (3%) relapse B-precursor ALLs. NT5C2 mutant proteins show increased nucleotidase activity in vitro and conferred resistance to chemotherapy with 6-mercaptopurine and 6-thioguanine (nucleoside-analog drugs) when expressed in ALL lymphoblasts. These results support a prominent role for activating mutations in NT5C2 and increased nucleoside-analog metabolism in disease progression and chemotherapy resistance in ALL.

A coding-independent function of gene and pseudogene mRNAs regulates tumour biology (The study explores how messenger RNAs (mRNAs) can have regulatory roles beyond just delivering protein-coding information). The canonical role of messenger RNA (mRNA) is to deliver protein-coding information to sites of protein synthesis (the process by which cells produce proteins from genetic instructions). However, given that microRNAs (miRNAs) bind to RNAs (ribonucleic acids), we hypothesized that RNAs could possess a regulatory role that relies on their ability to compete for miRNA binding, independently of their protein-coding function (the hypothesis suggests that RNAs might influence gene expression through interactions with miRNAs without necessarily encoding proteins). As a model for the protein-coding-independent role of RNAs, we describe the functional relationship between the mRNAs produced by the PTEN tumour suppressor gene (a gene that normally inhibits cell growth and division) and its pseudogene PTENP1 (a non-functional copy of PTEN) and the critical consequences of this interaction (we investigate how PTEN and PTENP1 interact and what effects this has on cell behavior). We find that PTENP1 is biologically active as it can regulate cellular levels of PTEN and exert a growth-suppressive role (PTENP1 can affect PTEN levels and inhibit cell growth). We also show that the PTENP1 locus is selectively lost in human cancer (PTENP1 is often missing in cancer cells). We extended our analysis to other cancer-related genes that possess pseudogenes, such as oncogenic KRAS (a gene that, when mutated, promotes uncontrolled cell growth) (for example, we looked at KRAS and its pseudogene). We also demonstrate that the transcripts of protein-coding genes such as PTEN are biologically active (the RNA copies of protein-coding genes like PTEN can have functional roles). These findings attribute a novel biological role to expressed pseudogenes, as they can regulate coding gene expression, and reveal a non-coding function for mRNAs (this research suggests that pseudogenes can control the activity of protein-coding genes and that mRNAs can have functions beyond just producing proteins).

Prostaglandin E2 (a lipid mediator involved in inflammation and pain) promotes intestinal tumor growth via DNA methylation (an epigenetic modification where methyl groups are added to DNA, typically suppressing gene expression). Although aberrant DNA methylation is considered to be one of the key ways by which tumor-suppressor and DNA-repair genes are silenced during tumor initiation and progression, the mechanisms underlying DNA methylation alterations in cancer remain unclear. Here we show that prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) silences certain tumor-suppressor and DNA-repair genes through DNA methylation to promote tumor growth. These findings uncover a previously unrecognized role for PGE2 (PGE2) in the promotion of tumor progression.

Auditory Cortex Tracks Both Auditory and Visual Stimulus Dynamics Using Low-Frequency Neuronal Phase Modulation (neurons modulating their firing patterns to carry information). Integrating information across sensory domains (combining data from different senses) to construct a unified representation of multi-sensory signals (combining audio and visual inputs into a coherent perception) is a fundamental characteristic of perception in ecological contexts (natural settings). One provocative hypothesis deriving from neurophysiology (study of nervous system function) suggests that there exists early and direct cross-modal phase modulation (neurons in one sensory area influencing neurons in another sensory area through phase changes). We provide evidence, based on magnetoencephalography (MEG) recordings (non-invasive brain imaging using magnetic fields) from participants viewing audiovisual movies, that low-frequency neuronal information (firing patterns in the 2-7 Hz range) lies at the basis of the synergistic coordination of information across auditory and visual streams (audio and visual signals working together). In particular, the phase of the 2-7 Hz delta and theta band responses (specific frequency bands of brain waves) carries robust (in single trials) and usable information (for parsing the temporal structure) about stimulus dynamics in both sensory modalities concurrently (audio and visual changes over time). These experiments are the first to show in humans that a particular cortical mechanism, delta-theta phase modulation across early sensory areas (neurons in auditory and visual cortex coordinating through phase changes), plays an important "active" role in continuously tracking naturalistic audio-visual streams (real-world audio and visual inputs), carrying dynamic multi-sensory information (audio and visual data combined), and reflecting cross-sensory interaction in real time (changes in one sense influencing perception in another sense as they happen).

Podoplanin-Rich Stromal Networks Induce Dendritic Cell Motility via Activation of the C-type Lectin Receptor CLEC-2 (C-type lectin domain-containing receptor involved in immune cell recognition) To initiate adaptive immunity, dendritic cells (DCs; specialized antigen-presenting cells) move from parenchymal tissues to lymphoid organs by migrating along stromal scaffolds that display the glycoprotein podoplanin (PDPN; a cell surface protein). PDPN is expressed by lymphatic endothelial and fibroblastic reticular cells and promotes blood-lymph separation during development by activating the C-type lectin receptor, CLEC-2, on platelets. Fate mapping reveals origins and dynamics of monocytes and tissue macrophages under homeostasis. Mononuclear phagocytes, including monocytes (a type of circulating white blood cell), macrophages (specialized immune cells that reside in tissues), and dendritic cells (antigen-presenting cells that activate T cells), contribute to tissue integrity as well as to innate and adaptive immune defense. Emerging evidence for labor division indicates that manipulation of these cells could bear therapeutic potential. However, specific ontogenies (developmental origins) of individual populations and the overall functional organization of this cellular network are not well defined. Here we report a fate-mapping study of the murine monocyte and macrophage compartment taking advantage of constitutive and conditional CX(3)CR1 promoter-driven Cre recombinase expression. We have demonstrated that major tissue-resident macrophage populations, including liver Kupffer cells (resident macrophages in the liver) and lung alveolar, splenic, and peritoneal macrophages (resident macrophages in the lungs, spleen, and abdominal cavity, respectively), are established prior to birth and maintain themselves subsequently during adulthood independent of replenishment by blood monocytes. Furthermore, we have established that short-lived Ly6C(+) monocytes constitute obligatory steady-state precursors of blood-resident Ly6C(-) cells (long-lived monocytes that do not express Ly6C marker) and that the abundance of Ly6C(+) blood monocytes dynamically controls the circulation lifespan of their progeny.

A Century of Cholesterol and Coronaries: From Plaques to Genes to Statins One-fourth of all deaths in industrialized countries result from coronary heart disease (CHD, a condition where arteries become narrowed due to plaque buildup, leading to reduced blood flow to the heart). A century of research has revealed the essential causative agent: cholesterol-carrying low-density lipoprotein (LDL, a type of lipoprotein that transports cholesterol in the bloodstream). LDL is controlled by specific receptors (LDLRs, Low-Density Lipoprotein Receptors) in liver that remove it from blood. Mutations that eliminate LDLRs raise LDL and cause heart attacks in childhood, whereas mutations that raise LDLRs reduce LDL and diminish heart attacks. If we are to eliminate coronary disease, lowering LDL should be the primary goal. Effective means to achieve this goal are currently available (statins, a class of drugs that lower LDL levels). The key questions are: who to treat, when to treat, and how long to treat.

To further clarify, statins inhibit HMG-CoA reductase (an enzyme involved in cholesterol synthesis), thereby reducing LDL production in the liver. The key questions remain: who to treat, when to initiate treatment, and for how long to continue treatment.

OBJECTIVES To explore the use of local civil registration data to assess the perinatal mortality in a typical rural county in a less developed province in China, 1999-2000. DESIGN Retrospective cohort study (a research design that examines outcomes of a group of individuals over time based on past records). Pregnancies in a cohort of women followed from registration of pregnancy to outcome of infant seven days after birth. SETTING Routine family planning records in 20 rural townships in eastern China. SUBJECTS 3697 pregnancies registered by the local family planning system during 1999. MAIN OUTCOME MEASURES Abortions, stillbirths, early neonatal mortality, perinatal mortality (death of an infant within one week after birth). RESULTS Only three cases were lost to follow up. The perinatal mortality rate was 69 per 1000 births, the rate of stillbirth was 24 per 1000 births, and the early neonatal mortality was 46 per 1000 live births. The early neonatal mortality was 29 in boys and 69 in girls per 1000 live births. The perinatal mortality rate increased notably with parity (number of previous births) and was higher in townships having lower income per capita. 7873737: Platelet glycoprotein (GP) IIb/IIIa inhibitors reduce mortality in diabetic patients with non-ST-segment-elevation acute coronary syndromes (ACS). BACKGROUND Diabetes mellitus (DM) is a major risk factor for adverse outcomes after acute coronary syndromes (ACS). Because this disease may be associated with increased platelet aggregation, we investigated whether diabetic patients with ACS derive particular benefit from platelet glycoprotein (GP) IIb/IIIa receptor inhibition. METHODS AND RESULTS We performed a meta-analysis of the diabetic populations enrolled in the 6 large-scale platelet GP IIb/IIIa inhibitor ACS trials: PRISM, PRISM-PLUS, PARAGON A, PARAGON B, PURSUIT, and GUSTO IV. Among 6458 diabetic patients, platelet GP IIb/IIIa inhibition was associated with a significant mortality reduction at 30 days, from 6.2% to 4.6% (OR 0.74; 95% CI 0.59 to 0.92; P=0.007). Conversely, 23 072 nondiabetic patients had no survival benefit (3.0% versus 3.0%). The interaction between platelet GP IIb/IIIa inhibition and diabetic status was statistically significant (P=0.036). Among 1279 diabetic patients undergoing percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) during index hospitalization, the use of these agents was associated with a mortality reduction at 30 days from 4.0% to 1.2% (OR 0.30; 95% CI 0.14 to 0.69; P=0.002). CONCLUSIONS This meta-analysis, including the entire large-scale trial experience of intravenous platelet GP IIb/IIIa inhibitors for the medical management of non-ST-segment-elevation ACS, Platelet glycoprotein (GP) IIb/IIIa inhibitors reduce mortality in diabetic patients with non-ST-segment-elevation acute coronary syndromes (ACS).

Cyclooxygenase-Dependent Tumor Growth through Evasion of Immunity The mechanisms by which melanoma and other cancer cells evade anti-tumor immunity remain incompletely understood (cancer cells avoid detection and destruction by the immune system). Here, we show that the growth of tumors formed by mutant Braf(V600E) mouse melanoma cells in an immunocompetent host requires their production of prostaglandin E2 (PGE2), which suppresses immunity and fuels tumor-promoting inflammation (PGE2 inhibits immune responses and promotes inflammation that supports tumor growth). Genetic ablation of cyclooxygenases (COX; enzymes that catalyze the rate-limiting step in PGE2 synthesis) or prostaglandin E synthases in Braf(V600E) mouse melanoma cells, as well as in Nras(G12D) melanoma or in breast or colorectal cancer cells, renders them susceptible to immune control and provokes a shift in the tumor inflammatory profile toward classic anti-cancer immune pathways (the absence of COX leads to a change in the tumor's inflammatory environment, making it more favorable for immune attack on the tumor). This mouse COX-dependent inflammatory signature is remarkably conserved in human cutaneous melanoma biopsies, arguing for COX activity as a driver of immune suppression across species (similar patterns of COX activity are observed in human melanomas, suggesting that COX plays a role in suppressing the immune response against tumors in humans too). Pre-clinical data demonstrate that inhibition of COX synergizes with anti-PD-1 blockade in inducing eradication of tumors (combining COX inhibition with PD-1 blockade enhances tumor elimination), implying that COX inhibitors could be useful adjuvants for immune-based therapies in cancer patients (COX inhibitors might improve the effectiveness of immune-based cancer treatments).

Scaffold-based three-dimensional human dermal fibroblast culture (3DFC) provides a structural matrix that supports angiogenesis (formation of new blood vessels) in infarcted heart tissue. BACKGROUND We have developed techniques to implant angiogenic patches onto the epicardium (outer surface of the heart) over regions of infarcted cardiac tissue to stimulate revascularization (restoration of blood supply) of the damaged tissue. These experiments used a scaffold-based 3D human dermal fibroblast culture (3DFC) as an epicardial patch. The 3DFC contains viable cells that secrete angiogenic growth factors (molecules that promote blood vessel formation) and has previously been shown to stimulate angiogenic activity. The hypothesis tested was that a viable 3DFC cardiac patch would stimulate an angiogenic response within an area of infarcted cardiac tissue. METHODS AND RESULTS A coronary occlusion of a branch of the left anterior descending coronary artery was performed by thermal ligation in severe combined immunodeficient mice. 3DFCs with or without viable cells were sized to the damaged area, implanted in replicate mice onto the epicardium at the site of tissue injury, and compared with animals that received infarct surgery but no implant. Fourteen and 30 days after surgery, hearts were exposed and photographed, and tissue samples were prepared for histology (examination of tissue under a microscope) and cytochemistry (chemical analysis of cellular components). Fourteen and 30 days after surgery, the damaged myocardium receiving viable 3DFC exhibited a significantly greater angiogenic response (including arterioles, venules, and capillaries) than nonviable and untreated control groups. CONCLUSIONS In this animal model, the use of a viable 3DFC cardiac patch demonstrated a robust angiogenic response in infarcted myocardium, suggesting potential therapeutic applications for treating heart disease.

Ribosome Profiling Provides Evidence that Large Noncoding RNAs Do Not Encode Proteins Large noncoding RNAs (lncRNAs, RNA molecules that do not code for proteins) are emerging as an important component in cellular regulation. Considerable evidence indicates that these transcripts act directly as functional RNAs rather than through an encoded protein product. However, a recent study of ribosome occupancy (the extent to which ribosomes are bound to RNA molecules) reported that many large intergenic noncoding RNAs (lincRNAs, lncRNAs transcribed from intergenic regions) are bound by ribosomes, raising the possibility that they are translated into proteins. Here, we show that classical noncoding RNAs and 5' untranslated regions (5' UTRs, the sequence at the beginning of a messenger RNA molecule before the coding region) show the same ribosome occupancy as lincRNAs, demonstrating that ribosome occupancy alone is not sufficient to classify transcripts as coding or noncoding. Instead, we define a metric based on the known property of translation (the process by which genetic information is used to synthesize proteins) whereby translating ribosomes are released upon encountering a bona fide stop codon (a specific sequence of nucleotides in mRNA that signals the termination of protein synthesis). We show that this metric accurately discriminates between protein-coding transcripts and all classes of known noncoding transcripts, including lincRNAs. Taken together, these results argue that the large majority of lincRNAs do not function through encoded proteins.

The Utilization of Extracellular Proteins as Nutrients Is Suppressed by mTORC1 (mechanistic target of rapamycin complex 1) Despite being surrounded by diverse nutrients, mammalian cells preferentially metabolize glucose and free amino acids. Recently, Ras-induced macropinocytosis of extracellular proteins was shown to reduce a transformed cell's dependence on extracellular glutamine (an amino acid). Here, we demonstrate that protein macropinocytosis can also serve as an essential amino acid source. Lysosomal degradation of extracellular proteins can sustain cell survival and induce activation of mTORC1 but fails to elicit significant cell accumulation. Unlike its growth-promoting activity under amino-acid-replete conditions, we discovered that mTORC1 activation suppresses proliferation when cells rely on extracellular proteins as an amino acid source. Inhibiting mTORC1 results in increased catabolism of endocytosed proteins and enhances cell proliferation during nutrient-depleted conditions in vitro and within vascularly compromised tumors in vivo. Thus, by preventing nutritional consumption of extracellular proteins, mTORC1 couples growth to availability of free amino acids. These results may have important implications for the use of mTOR inhibitors as therapeutics.

Mitochondria: Dynamic Organelles in Disease, Aging, and Development
Mitochondria are the primary energy-generating system in most eukaryotic cells (cells with a nucleus enclosed within a membrane). Additionally, they participate in intermediary metabolism (the series of chemical reactions by which a cell builds up or breaks down molecules), calcium signaling (processes involving the release and uptake of calcium ions to regulate cellular activities), and apoptosis (programmed cell death). Given these well-established functions, it might be expected that mitochondrial dysfunction would give rise to a simple and predictable set of defects in all tissues. However, mitochondrial dysfunction has pleiotropic effects (affecting multiple unrelated traits) in multicellular organisms. Clearly, much about the basic biology of mitochondria remains to be understood. Here we discuss recent work that suggests that the dynamics (fusion and fission) of these organelles is important in development and disease.

Identification and Functional Characterization of N-Terminally Acetylated Proteins in Drosophila melanogaster Protein modifications play a major role for most biological processes in living organisms (e.g., signaling, regulation, structural changes). Amino-terminal acetylation of proteins is a common modification found throughout the tree of life: the N-terminus of a nascent polypeptide chain becomes co-translationally acetylated, often after the removal of the initiating methionine residue (Met). While the enzymes and protein complexes involved in these processes have been extensively studied, only little is known about the biological function of such N-terminal modification events. To identify common principles of N-terminal acetylation, we analyzed the amino-terminal peptides from proteins extracted from Drosophila Kc167 cells. We detected more than 1,200 mature protein N-termini and could show that N-terminal acetylation occurs in insects with a similar frequency as in humans. As the sole true determinant for N-terminal acetylation we could extract the (X)PX rule that indicates the prevention of acetylation under all circumstances (i.e., when the N-terminus follows a specific sequence pattern). We could show that this rule can be used to genetically engineer a protein to study the biological relevance of the presence or absence of an acetyl group, thereby generating a generic assay to probe the functional importance of N-terminal acetylation. We applied the assay by expressing mutated proteins as transgenes in cell lines and in flies. Here, we present a straightforward strategy to systematically
augment the study.

PU.1-mediated upregulation of CSF1R (colony-stimulating factor 1 receptor) is crucial for leukemia stem cell potential induced by MOZ-TIF2 (monocytic leukemia zinc finger-TIF2 fusion protein) Leukemias and other cancers possess self-renewing stem cells that help to maintain the cancer (cancer stem cells). Eradication of cancer stem cells is thought to be crucial for successful anticancer therapy. Using an acute myeloid leukemia (AML) model induced by the leukemia-associated monocytic leukemia zinc finger (MOZ)-TIF2 fusion protein, we show here that AML can be cured by the ablation of leukemia stem cells. The MOZ fusion proteins MOZ-TIF2 and MOZ-CBP (cyclic adenosine monophosphate response element-binding protein) interacted with the transcription factor PU.1 (PU.1, a master regulator of hematopoiesis) to stimulate the expression of macrophage colony–stimulating factor receptor (CSF1R, also known as M-CSFR, c-FMS or CD115). Studies using PU.1-deficient mice showed that PU.1 is essential for the ability of MOZ-TIF2 to establish and maintain AML stem cells. Cells expressing high amounts of CSF1R (CSF1Rhigh cells), but not those expressing low amounts of CSF1R (CSF1Rlow cells), showed potent leukemia-initiating activity. Using transgenic mice expressing a drug-inducible suicide gene controlled by the CSF1R promoter, we cured AML by ablation of CSF1Rhigh cells. Moreover, induction of AML was suppressed in CSF1R-deficient mice and CSF1R inhibitors slowed the progression of MOZ-TIF2–induced leukemia. Thus, in this subtype of AML, leukemia stem cells are contained within the CSF1Rhigh cell population, and we suggest that targeting of PU.1-mediated upregulation of CSF1R expression might be a useful therapeutic approach.

Deregulation of Scribble (a protein involved in establishing and maintaining cell polarity) promotes mammary tumorigenesis (the development of tumors in the breast) and reveals a role for cell polarity in carcinoma (cancer characterized by the uncontrolled growth and spread of abnormal cells). Loss of cell polarity proteins such as Scribble (a key regulator of cell shape and organization) induces neoplasia (abnormal tissue growth) in Drosophila (fruit flies) by promoting uncontrolled proliferation (rapid cell division). In mammals, the role that polarity proteins play during tumorigenesis is not well understood. Here, we demonstrate that depletion of Scribble in mammary epithelia (cells lining the milk-producing glands) disrupts cell polarity, blocks three-dimensional morphogenesis (the process by which tissues and organs are formed), inhibits apoptosis (programmed cell death) (p < 0.01, statistically significant), and induces dysplasia (abnormal tissue growth) in vivo (within the body) that progress to tumors after long latency (delayed onset). Loss of Scribble cooperates with oncogenes such as c-myc (genes that can cause normal cells to become cancerous when mutated) to transform epithelial cells (cells that line surfaces and organs) and induce tumors in vivo (within the body) by blocking activation of an apoptosis pathway (the process by which cells are eliminated from the body). Like depletion, mislocalization of Scribble from cell-cell junctions (locations where adjacent cells meet and interact) was sufficient to promote cell transformation. Interestingly, spontaneous mammary tumors in mice and humans possess both downregulated and mislocalized Scribble. Thus, we demonstrate that Scribble inhibits breast cancer formation and that deregulation of polarity pathways promotes dysplastic and neoplastic growth in mammals by disrupting morphogenesis (the process by which tissues and organs are formed) and inhibiting cell death.

The Polymeal: a more natural, safer, and probably tastier (than the Polypill) strategy to reduce cardiovascular disease by more than 75%. OBJECTIVE Although the Polypill concept (proposed in 2003) is promising in terms of benefits for cardiovascular risk management, the potential costs and adverse effects are its main pitfalls. The objective of this study was to identify a tastier and safer alternative to the Polypill: the Polymeal. METHODS Data on the ingredients of the Polymeal were taken from the literature. The evidence-based recipe included wine (contains polyphenols), fish (rich in omega-3 fatty acids), dark chocolate (high in flavonoids), fruits (contain vitamins and antioxidants), vegetables (rich in fiber and phytochemicals), garlic (has allicin, a compound with anti-inflammatory properties), and almonds (a good source of monounsaturated fats). Data from the Framingham heart study and the Framingham offspring study were used to build life tables to model the benefits of the Polymeal in the general population from age 50, assuming multiplicative correlations. RESULTS Combining the ingredients of the Polymeal would reduce cardiovascular disease events by 76%. For men, taking the Polymeal daily represented an increase in total life expectancy of 6.6 years, an increase in life expectancy free from cardiovascular disease of 9.0 years, and a decrease in life expectancy with cardiovascular disease of 2.4 years. The corresponding differences for women were 4.8, 8.1, and 3.3 years. CONCLUSION The Polymeal promises to be an effective, non-pharmacological, safe, cheap, and tasty alternative to reduce cardiovascular morbidity and increase life expectancy in the general population.

Arp2/3 complex inhibition radically alters lamellipodial (LP: protrusion at the leading edge of migrating cells) actin architecture, suspended cell shape, and the cell spreading process. Recent studies have investigated the dendritic actin cytoskeleton of the cell edge's lamellipodial (LP: protrusion at the leading edge of migrating cells) region by experimentally decreasing the activity of the actin filament nucleator and branch former, the Arp2/3 complex (a protein complex that nucleates and branches actin filaments). Here we extend these studies via pharmacological inhibition of the Arp2/3 complex in sea urchin coelomocytes (cells from the coelom of sea urchins), cells that possess an unusually broad LP region (protrusion at the leading edge) and display correspondingly exaggerated centripetal flow (flow towards the center of the cell). Using light and electron microscopy, we demonstrate that Arp2/3 complex inhibition via the drug CK666 (a chemical compound used to inhibit the Arp2/3 complex) dramatically altered LP actin architecture, slowed centripetal flow, drove a lamellipodial-to-filopodial shape change in suspended cells (change from a broad lamellipodium to a more slender filopodium), and induced a novel actin structural organization during cell spreading. A general feature of the CK666 phenotype in coelomocytes was transverse actin arcs (horizontal actin structures), and arc generation was arrested by a formin inhibitor (a chemical compound that inhibits the formin proteins involved in actin filament formation). We also demonstrate that CK666 treatment produces actin arcs in other cells with broad LP regions, namely fish keratocytes (cells from the cornea of fish) and Drosophila S2 cells (Drosophila Schneider cell line, commonly used in genetic research). We hypothesize that the actin arcs made visible by Arp2/3 complex inhibition in coelomocytes may represent an exaggerated manifestation of the elongate mother filaments (longer actin filaments) that could possibly serve as the scaffold for the production of the dendritic actin network (branching actin network).

T cell receptor (TCR) signaling is initiated and sustained in microclusters; however, it's not known whether signaling also occurs in the TCR-rich central supramolecular activation cluster (cSMAC). We showed that the cSMAC formed by fusion of microclusters contained more CD45 (receptor tyrosine phosphatase) than microclusters and is a site enriched in lysobisphosphatidic acid (LPA), a lipid involved in sorting ubiquitinated membrane proteins for degradation. Calcium signaling via TCR was blocked within 2 min by anti-MHCp (major histocompatibility complex class I polypeptide-related sequence A) treatment and 1 min by latrunculin-A (a drug that disrupts actin filaments) treatment. TCR-MHCp (major histocompatibility complex class I polypeptide) interactions in the cSMAC survived these perturbations for 10 min and hence were not sufficient to sustain signaling. TCR microclusters were also resistant to disruption by anti-MHCp and latrunculin-A treatments. We propose that TCR signaling is sustained by stabilized microclusters and is terminated in the cSMAC, a structure from which TCR are sorted for degradation. Our studies reveal a role for F-actin (filamentous actin) in TCR signaling beyond microcluster formation.

Differential innate immune response programs in neuronal subtypes determine susceptibility to infection in the brain by positive-stranded RNA viruses (viruses that replicate using a single-stranded RNA molecule as a template). Although susceptibility of neurons in the brain to microbial infection is a major determinant of clinical outcome, little is known about the molecular factors governing this vulnerability. Here we show that two types of neurons from distinct brain regions (cerebellar granule cell neurons and cortical neurons) showed differential permissivity to replication of several positive-stranded RNA viruses. Granule cell neurons of the cerebellum and cortical neurons from the cerebral cortex have unique innate immune programs (the set of genes and proteins that help the body fight off infections) that confer differential susceptibility to viral infection ex vivo (outside a living organism) and in vivo (inside a living organism). By transducing cortical neurons with genes that were expressed more highly in granule cell neurons, we identified three interferon-stimulated genes (ISGs; Ifi27 (interferon-induced protein with tetratricopeptide repeats 27), Irg1 (immunoglobulin domain and RNase domains containing 1), and Rsad2 (also known as Viperin (vesicular stomatitis virus-induced protein)) that mediated the antiviral effects against different neurotropic viruses. Moreover, we found that the epigenetic state (changes in gene expression that do not involve alterations to the DNA sequence) and microRNA (miRNA)-mediated regulation of ISGs correlates with enhanced antiviral response in granule cell neurons. Thus, neurons from evolutionarily distinct brain regions have unique innate immune signatures (characteristic patterns of gene expression and protein activity), which probably contribute to their relative permissiveness to infection.

Senescent Cells, Tumor Suppression, and Organismal Aging: Good Citizens, Bad Neighbors Cells from organisms with renewable tissues can permanently withdraw from the cell cycle in response to diverse stress, including dysfunctional telomeres (DNA sequences at the ends of chromosomes that shorten with each cell division), DNA damage, strong mitogenic signals (stimuli that promote cell division), and disrupted chromatin (DNA packaging material within the nucleus). This response, termed cellular senescence, is controlled by the p53 (tumor suppressor protein that monitors DNA integrity and triggers cell cycle arrest or apoptosis if necessary) and RB (retinoblastoma protein, another tumor suppressor that regulates the cell cycle) tumor suppressor proteins and constitutes a potent anticancer mechanism. Nonetheless, senescent cells acquire phenotypic changes that may contribute to aging and certain age-related diseases, including late-life cancer. Thus, the senescence response may be antagonistically pleiotropic, promoting early-life survival by curtailing the development of cancer but eventually limiting longevity as dysfunctional senescent cells accumulate.

Increasing microtubule acetylation rescues axonal transport and locomotor deficits caused by LRRK2 Roc-COR domain mutations (Leucine-rich repeat kinase 2 (LRRK2) mutations are the most common genetic cause of Parkinson's disease (PD)). LRRK2 is a multifunctional protein affecting many cellular processes (including regulation of microtubule dynamics) and has been described to bind microtubules (filamentous structures that provide structural support and facilitate intracellular transport). Defective microtubule-based axonal transport (the movement of vesicles and organelles along microtubules within neuronal axons) is hypothesized to contribute to Parkinson's disease, but whether LRRK2 mutations affect this process to mediate pathogenesis is not known. Here we find that LRRK2 containing pathogenic Roc-COR domain mutations (R1441C, Y1699C) preferentially associates with deacetylated microtubules (microtubules with reduced acetylation levels), and inhibits axonal transport (movement of cargo along axons) in primary neurons (neurons isolated from living organisms) and in Drosophila (fruit flies), causing locomotor deficits (movement impairments) in vivo (in living organisms). In vitro (in laboratory conditions outside of living organisms), increasing microtubule acetylation using deacetylase inhibitors (drugs that inhibit enzymes responsible for removing acetyl groups from proteins) or the tubulin acetyltransferase αTAT1 (an enzyme that adds acetyl groups to tubulin subunits) prevents association of mutant LRRK2 with microtubules, and the deacetylase inhibitor trichostatin A (TSA) (a specific inhibitor of histone deacetylases) restores axonal transport. In vivo (in living organisms), knockdown of the deacetylases HDAC6 and Sirt2 (enzymes that remove acetyl groups from proteins) or administration of TSA rescues both axonal transport and locomotor behavior. Thus, this study reveals a pathogenic mechanism (mechanism by which LRRK2 mutations lead to disease) and a potential intervention (therapeutic approach) for Parkinson's disease.

Genome-wide association analyses in East Asians identify new susceptibility loci for colorectal cancer (CRC). To identify new genetic factors for colorectal cancer (CRC), we conducted a genome-wide association study (GWAS) in east Asians. By analyzing genome-wide data in 2,098 cases and 5,749 controls, we selected 64 promising single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) for replication in an independent set of samples, including up to 5,358 cases and 5,922 controls. We identified four SNPs with association P values of 8.58 × 10(-7) to 3.77 × 10(-10) in the combined analysis of all east Asian samples. Three of the four were replicated in a study conducted in 26,060 individuals of European descent, with combined P values of 1.22 × 10(-10) for rs647161 (5q31.1), 6.64 × 10(-9) for rs2423279 (20p12.3) and 3.06 × 10(-8) for rs10774214 (12p13.32 near the cyclin D2 gene, CCND2), derived from meta-analysis of data from both east Asian and European-ancestry populations. This study identified three new CRC susceptibility loci and provides additional insight into the genetics and biology of colorectal cancer (CRC).

OBJECTIVE To investigate the long-term effect of radioactive iodine (radioactive isotope of iodine used in nuclear medicine) on thyroid function and size in patients with non-toxic multinodular goitre. DESIGN Consecutive patients with multinodular non-toxic goitre selected for radioactive iodine treatment and followed for a minimum of 12 months (median 48 months) after an intended dose of 3.7 MBq/g thyroid tissue corrected to a 100% uptake of iodine-131 (radioactive isotope of iodine) in 24 hours. PATIENTS 69 patients with a growing multinodular non-toxic goitre (goitre with multiple nodules that do not cause hyperthyroidism) causing local compression symptoms or cosmetic inconveniences. The treatment was chosen because of a high operative risk, previous thyroidectomy, or refusal to be operated on. MAIN OUTCOME MEASUREMENTS Standard thyroid function variables and ultrasonically determined thyroid volume before treatment as well as 1, 2, 3, 6, and 12 months after treatment and then once a year. RESULTS 56 patients were treated with a single dose of 131I (radioactive isotope of iodine), 12 with two doses, and one with four doses. In 45 patients treated with one dose and remaining euthyroid (having normal thyroid function), the median thyroid volume was reduced from 73 (interquartile range 50-106) ml to 29 (23-48) ml at 24 months in the 39 patients in whom this was measured during follow-up. The median reduction was 40 (22-48) ml (60% reduction, p < 0.0001), half of which occurred within three months. Patients treated with two doses as well as those developing hypothyroidism (underactive thyroid) and hyperthyroidism (overactive thyroid) had a significant reduction in thyroid volume.

Human papillomavirus (HPV, a virus that can cause changes in cervical cells leading to cancer) testing for the detection of high-grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia (CIN, abnormal cell growth in the cervix that may progress to cancer) and cancer: final results of the POBASCAM (Prevalence Of Basal Cell Adenocarcinoma in Screening for Cervical Malignancy) randomised controlled trial. BACKGROUND Human papillomavirus (HPV) testing is more sensitive for the detection of high-grade cervical lesions (abnormal tissue growth) than is cytology (examination of cells under a microscope), but detection of HPV by DNA screening in two screening rounds 5 years apart has not been assessed. The aim of this study was to assess whether HPV DNA testing in the first screen decreases detection of cervical intraepithelial neoplasia (CIN, abnormal cell growth in the cervix that may progress to cancer) grade 3 or worse, CIN grade 2 or worse, and cervical cancer in the second screening. METHODS In this randomised trial, women aged 29-56 years participating in the cervical screening programme in the Netherlands were randomly assigned to receive HPV DNA (GP5+/6+-PCR method, a technique to detect HPV DNA) and cytology co-testing or cytology testing alone, from January, 1999, to September, 2002. Randomisation (in a 1:1 ratio) was done with computer-generated random numbers after the cervical specimen had been taken. At the second screening 5 years later, HPV DNA and cytology co-testing was done in both groups; researchers were masked to the patient's assignment. The primary endpoint was the number of CIN grade 3 or worse detected. Analysis was done by intention to screen. The trial is now finished and is registered, number ISRCTN20781131. Epigenetic therapy activates type I interferon (IFN) signaling in murine ovarian cancer to reduce immunosuppression and tumor burden. Ovarian cancer is the most lethal of all gynecological cancers, and there is an urgent unmet need to develop new therapies. Epithelial ovarian cancer (EOC) is characterized by an immune suppressive microenvironment (tumor environment that discourages immune responses), and response of ovarian cancers to immune therapies has thus far been disappointing. We now find, in a mouse model of EOC, that clinically relevant doses of DNA methyltransferase and histone deacetylase inhibitors (DNMTi and HDACi, respectively) reduce the immune suppressive microenvironment through type I IFN signaling and improve response to immune checkpoint therapy. These data indicate that the type I IFN response is required for effective in vivo antitumorigenic actions of the DNMTi 5-azacytidine (AZA). Through type I IFN signaling, AZA increases the numbers of CD45+ immune cells and the percentage of active CD8+ T and natural killer (NK) cells in the tumor microenvironment, while reducing tumor burden and extending survival. AZA also increases viral defense gene expression in both tumor and immune cells, and reduces the percentage of macrophages and myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs) in the tumor microenvironment. The addition of an HDACi to AZA enhances the modulation of the immune microenvironment, specifically increasing T and NK cell activation and reducing macrophages over AZA treatment alone, while further increasing survival.

Epigenetic Therapy Ties MYC Depletion to Reversing Immune Evasion and Treating Lung Cancer Combining DNA-demethylating agents (DNA methyltransferase inhibitors [DNMTis]) with histone deacetylase inhibitors (HDACis) holds promise for enhancing cancer immune therapy. Herein, pharmacologic and isoform specificity of HDACis (enzymes that remove acetyl groups from histone proteins, regulating gene expression) are investigated to guide their addition to a DNMTi (enzymes that add methyl groups to DNA, inhibiting gene expression), thus devising a new, low-dose, sequential regimen that imparts a robust anti-tumor effect for non-small-cell lung cancer (NSCLC, a type of lung cancer). Using in-vitro-treated NSCLC cell lines, we elucidate an interferon α/β-based transcriptional program (a set of genes activated by interferons) with accompanying upregulation of antigen presentation machinery (proteins that display fragments of antigens on cell surfaces for immune recognition), mediated in part through double-stranded RNA (dsRNA, a type of RNA with two complementary strands) induction. This is accompanied by suppression of MYC signaling (MYC, a transcription factor that regulates cell growth and proliferation; its depletion can inhibit cancer cell growth) and an increase in the T cell chemoattractant CCL5 (a chemokine that attracts T cells to sites of inflammation or infection). Use of this combination treatment schema in mouse models of NSCLC reverses tumor immune evasion and modulates T cell exhaustion state towards memory and effector T cell phenotypes (T cells that have been activated and are ready to fight infections or tumors). Key correlative science metrics emerge for an upcoming clinical trial, testing enhancement of immune checkpoint therapy for NSCLC.
